title.en: Amazon Bedrock: Enterprise GenAI with Guardrails & Agents

description.en: Deploy generative AI the enterprise way: Bedrock's model marketplace, Knowledge Bases for RAG, Agents for tool use, and Guardrails as the policy layer — with SageMaker's role alongside.

story_variants.en:
## GenAI Without Handing Your Data to the Internet

When enterprises evaluate generative AI, the first three questions are never about model quality. They are: where does my prompt data go, who can audit it, and what stops the model from saying something that ends up in a regulator's exhibit list? **Amazon Bedrock** is AWS's answer: a serverless API over multiple foundation model families — Anthropic Claude, Amazon's own Nova models, Meta Llama, Mistral, Cohere, Stability and others — inside your AWS boundary. The enterprise-critical defaults: **your prompts and completions are not used to train the base models**, traffic can stay on **PrivateLink** (no internet path), everything is IAM-authorized and CloudTrail-logged, and content can be encrypted with your KMS keys. GenAI becomes another governed AWS workload instead of a shadow-IT SaaS subscription.

## The Building Blocks

- **Model access and inference.** Invoke models via a uniform API (`InvokeModel` / `Converse`), streaming or batch. Pricing model: **on-demand per token** (input and output priced separately) for most use; **provisioned throughput** buys dedicated model units for guaranteed capacity model-heavy production; **batch inference** trades latency for a discounted rate. Cross-region inference profiles spread load for resilience.
- **Knowledge Bases** — managed **RAG** (retrieval-augmented generation): point at S3 documents, Bedrock handles chunking, embedding (e.g., Titan or Cohere embeddings), and storage in a vector store (OpenSearch Serverless, Aurora pgvector, Pinecone and others), then retrieval-and-generate with citations. RAG grounds answers in *your* documents — the standard cure for hallucinated policy answers, and vastly cheaper than fine-tuning for knowledge injection.
- **Agents** — orchestration: the model plans, calls your **action groups** (Lambda-backed tools defined by OpenAPI schemas), consults knowledge bases, and loops until done. Think 'LLM with permission-scoped hands'. Multi-agent collaboration lets a supervisor agent route work to specialists.
- **Guardrails** — the policy layer, applied to inputs and outputs *independently of which model* is behind them: denied topics (natural-language definitions), content filters (hate/violence/sexual/insults thresholds), word filters, **PII detection with masking or blocking**, and **contextual grounding checks** that flag answers unsupported by retrieved sources. One guardrail, every model, consistently — which is exactly how enterprise policy wants to work.
- **Model customization** — fine-tuning and continued pre-training on labeled data in S3, producing private custom models. Also **model distillation** and prompt-management/evaluation tooling for the LLMOps loop.

## Where SageMaker Fits

**SageMaker AI** is the deeper ML platform: notebooks, training jobs on GPU fleets, experiment tracking, pipelines, model registry, and real-time/serverless endpoints. The clean division: **Bedrock when you consume foundation models via API; SageMaker when you build, train, or host your own models** (including open weights with custom serving). SageMaker JumpStart overlaps at the edges (deployable foundation models), and mature shops use both — Bedrock for the assistant products, SageMaker for the fraud model that is the actual crown jewels.

> **War story:** An insurer launched a claims-assistant chatbot straight to production on a raw model endpoint. Within a week it had confidently invented a nonexistent 'flood rider clause 4.2', and a screenshot reached compliance before engineering. The rebuild: Knowledge Base over the actual policy corpus, guardrail with denied topics ('legal advice', 'coverage guarantees'), PII masking on inputs, and contextual grounding checks — with citations rendered in the UI. Trust returned only after users could click the source. RAG plus guardrails is not decoration; it is the difference between a demo and a product.

## The Cost Model Nobody Budgets Correctly

Token economics invert normal capacity planning: cost scales with **conversation verbosity**, not user count. The levers, in order of impact:

| Lever | Effect |
|---|---|
| Model right-sizing | Small/fast models (Haiku-class, Nova Lite-class) for classification and routing; large models only where reasoning demands it |
| Prompt caching | Reuse of repeated prefixes (system prompts, documents) at reduced input rates |
| Output length limits | Output tokens typically price several times input tokens |
| Batch inference | Discounted rate for non-interactive workloads |
| RAG chunk discipline | Retrieval stuffing 20 chunks into context pays for 20 chunks every call |

A router pattern — cheap model classifies, expensive model handles the hard 10% — routinely cuts spend dramatically at equal quality. Track cost per *conversation*, not per token, and tag Bedrock usage with cost-allocation tags and application inference profiles like any other workload.

## Decision Framework: Shipping a GenAI Feature

1. **Start with the Converse API + the smallest model that passes your eval set.** Build the eval set first — 50 real prompts with graded answers beat vibes.
2. **Knowledge injection? RAG before fine-tuning**, always. Fine-tune for style, format and domain vocabulary, not facts that change weekly.
3. **Guardrails from day one**, tuned per surface: a customer-facing bot needs strict topics and PII masking; an internal dev tool needs less.
4. **Agents get least-privilege action groups** — the Lambda behind a tool runs with a scoped role; the agent cannot exceed what its tools' IAM allows. Treat prompt injection as untrusted input attacking your tools.
5. **Log everything** (model invocation logging to S3/CloudWatch) — evals, incident forensics, and cost archaeology all read the same logs.
6. **Own models or heavy training pipelines? SageMaker.** Consuming intelligence? Bedrock.

> **Pitfall:** Skipping the evaluation harness. Teams A/B their buttons but ship prompt changes to production untested. Bedrock's evaluation tooling (and plain scheduled test suites) exist so a prompt tweak that tanks grounding accuracy is caught in CI, not in a customer screenshot.

Generative AI in the enterprise is 20% model choice and 80% the boring perimeter: identity, encryption, logging, guardrails, evals, cost tags. Bedrock's real product is that perimeter — the same disciplines this track has been teaching all along, applied to a new kind of compute.

description.en: The governed GenAI request path: guardrails screen both directions, the agent orchestrates tools and retrieval, and every hop is IAM-scoped and logged.

left.label: Raw model endpoint answering policy questions from memory

right.label: RAG over the real corpus + guardrails + citations in the UI

description.en: Ungrounded models invent plausible clauses; Knowledge Bases retrieve real ones and contextual grounding checks flag the difference.

animation_sequence.code:
resp = bedrock.converse(
    modelId='anthropic.claude-3-5-haiku-20241022-v1:0',
    messages=[{'role': 'user',
               'content': [{'text': prompt}]}],
    guardrailConfig={'guardrailIdentifier': gr_id,
                     'guardrailVersion': '2'})

explanation.en: Model choice is a parameter, not an architecture. Route cheap prompts to small models; reserve large ones for hard reasoning.
The guardrail is attached at invocation — denied topics, content filters, and PII masking apply regardless of which model runs.
The Converse API is uniform across model families — swapping vendors is a string change plus an eval run, not a rewrite.

description.en: A guardrail intervening on PII before the prompt ever reaches a model — policy enforced as infrastructure, not as a system-prompt plea.
The managed RAG pipeline inside Knowledge Bases — knowledge injection without fine-tuning.

instruction.en: Build a Bedrock-style guardrail simulator — the input-screening pass that runs before any model sees a prompt. Write check_prompt(text) that returns: 'BLOCKED (pii)' if the text matches the US-SSN pattern \d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4} (use the re module), else 'BLOCKED (denied_topic)' if any word in DENIED_WORDS appears in the lowercased text, else 'ALLOWED'. Also write count_tokens(text) returning len(text.split()) — a rough proxy for billable input size. For each (name, text) in PROMPTS print exactly: for allowed prompts '<name> -> ALLOWED tokens=<n>', and for blocked ones '<name> -> <verdict>'. Finish with 'allowed=<a> blocked=<b>'. PII order matters: check PII before denied topics, exactly as layered policy filters do.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
import re

DENIED_WORDS = ["password", "exploit"]

PROMPTS = [
    ("prompt-1", "Summarize the quarterly revenue report for the board"),
    ("prompt-2", "My SSN is 123-45-6789 please store it"),
    ("prompt-3", "What is the admin password for the database"),
]

SSN_PATTERN = r"\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}"

def check_prompt(text):
    # TODO: if re.search(SSN_PATTERN, text): return "BLOCKED (pii)"
    # TODO: if any denied word in text.lower(): return "BLOCKED (denied_topic)"
    # TODO: return "ALLOWED"
    pass

def count_tokens(text):
    # TODO: whitespace token count
    pass

# TODO: for each (name, text): print verdict line
#   ALLOWED  -> f"{name} -> ALLOWED tokens={count_tokens(text)}"
#   blocked  -> f"{name} -> {verdict}"
# TODO: print f"allowed={a} blocked={b}"
practice_challenges.expected_output:
prompt-1 -> ALLOWED tokens=8
prompt-2 -> BLOCKED (pii)
prompt-3 -> BLOCKED (denied_topic)
allowed=1 blocked=2

en: re.search(SSN_PATTERN, text) is truthy when the pattern appears anywhere — no anchors needed.
Denied-topic check: any(w in text.lower() for w in DENIED_WORDS). Check PII first so prompt-2 reports pii, not a topic.
prompt-1 splits into exactly 8 whitespace tokens. Count verdicts with a==1, b==2 for the summary line.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert check_prompt("My SSN is 123-45-6789") == "BLOCKED (pii)"
assert check_prompt("what is the PASSWORD") == "BLOCKED (denied_topic)"
assert check_prompt("Summarize the meeting") == "ALLOWED"
assert check_prompt("password and 111-22-3333") == "BLOCKED (pii)"
assert count_tokens("a b  c") == 3
assert count_tokens("Summarize the quarterly revenue report for the board") == 8

q.en: An enterprise needs a chatbot to answer questions from its own policy documents, updated weekly. What is the recommended Bedrock approach?

en: Fine-tune the model on the documents every week
Knowledge Bases (RAG) — retrieval grounds answers in the current documents with citations
Increase the model temperature for more creative answers
Continued pre-training on the corporate wiki

q.en: What is the key architectural property of Bedrock Guardrails?

en: They apply consistently to inputs and outputs independently of which foundation model is used
They only work with Amazon-built models
They replace IAM for Bedrock APIs
They are enforced by the model's system prompt

q.en: When should a team choose SageMaker over Bedrock?

en: When training or hosting their own custom models with full control of the training pipeline

q.en: Which practice most reduces token spend for a high-volume assistant at equal quality?

en: Always using the largest model for consistency
A router pattern: a small model handles routine prompts, escalating hard cases to a large model
